narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background brings unique strengths and challenges that the admissions model carefully considered alongside your academic record. The model predicts a 65.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This decision hinged most on your performance rankings in school, particularly your Year 3 decile of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of 5.71, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, signaling to the model a pattern of underperformance relative to peers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 30 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing success compared to the dataset average of 36.70, though it wasn't enough to offset the other influences. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 (below the 3.21 average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often rank higher in school deciles and score better on the LSAT—your profile stood out as riskier for bar passage, especially given how common full-time enrollment is (over 92% of students). This isn't a judgment on your potential or the hurdles you may have faced as a Hispanic applicant; it's the model highlighting where your record aligns less favorably with proven bar passers. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or retaking key assessments, while considering LSAT prep to strengthen that positive factor further. Retaking the LSAT or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to improve your GPA could reshape your profile for future applications. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and reapply once you've built a stronger case. Your determination as a male from a middle-income Hispanic background positions you well to overcome this and thrive in law.
